faqs.q: Is people-pleasing a mental health diagnosis?
faqs.a: No, people-pleasing isn't a clinical diagnosis. It's a learned behavior pattern of prioritizing others' approval over your own needs, and it can overlap with low self-esteem and anxiety.

faqs.q: What's the difference between people-pleasing and just being kind?
faqs.a: Kindness comes from choice and still leaves room for your own needs, while people-pleasing is driven more by fear of disapproval and tends to crowd out your own limits. One adds to your life, the other can slowly drain it.

Yes, because it's a learned pattern rather than a fixed trait, it can be unlearned over time. Building boundaries, practicing saying no, and working on the beliefs underneath it, often in therapy, can shift the pattern.
